user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant's default. The suit claims total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act and demands a refund with interest and costs.

slots.value: Recovery of advance with interest and costs

missing_fields: Date of Defendant's default / failure of consideration
assumptions: Jurisdiction lies with the specified District Court based on cause of action or defendant residence
Suit is within the limitation period from the date of failure of consideration

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, employed as {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}} and residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to sue in this action. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, employed as {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}} and residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is the Opposite Party to whom the Plaintiff seeks relief in this suit.

On {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} the Plaintiff and Defendant entered into an Agreement evidenced by Annexure A, whereby the Defendant undertook to repay the amount advanced by the Plaintiff and to perform the specified obligations by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Plaintiff, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, made an advance payment to the Defendant, as evidenced by Annexure B. The Defendant was obligated to perform by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} but failed to repay or perform by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, thereby breaching the contract and giving rise to this action. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}.

Following the breach, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice on {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding payment; the Defendant replied on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}; and the Plaintiff filed this suit on {{FILING_DATE}} seeking recovery of the due amount and costs, with reliance on Annexure C (Legal Notice), Annexure D (Reply, if any), Annexure E (Acknowledgment/Admission, if any), and Annexure F (Additional supporting documents).

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff and the Defendant came to know each other in the course of pursuing a commercial opportunity, culminating in a formal written agreement between them on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, as evidenced by Annexure A. Their relationship was that of contracting parties to a business transaction, with the Plaintiff proposing and the Defendant evaluating a venture and entering into a binding contract to govern their dealings.

Under the terms of the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff agreed to provide an advance of Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant for the purpose of effecting the business arrangement described in Annexure A. The terms contemplated that, if the transaction could not be carried through, the Defendant would refund the advance to the Plaintiff. The agreement sets out the conditions and remedies for non-performance and references the obligations of both sides to ensure the advance would be used as contemplated or returned if the transaction did not materialize.

The obligations laid down in the contract were that the Plaintiff would disburse the advance amount on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and that the Defendant would perform the required duties and deliver the goods or services by the date specified as {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The parties understood that, in the event the transaction could not be completed, the Defendant would refund the entire advance amount within the period and manner described in the agreement. Annexure A contains the operative terms and the referenced performance timetable.

On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff remitted Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant by bank transfer, as evidenced by the payment proof in Annexure B. The transfer consisted of the full advance amount and was sent to the Defendant in accordance with the details set out in Annexure B, which also includes the date and method of payment. This payment formed the consideration for the contemplated transaction under the Agreement and was intended to be applied to the transaction as described in Annexure A.

By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant did not perform the contractual obligations, and the refund of the advance was not effected. The goods or services were not delivered as contemplated, and the advance money remained with the Defendant without the required return to the Plaintiff as demanded by the terms of the Agreement. The date {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} marks when the Plaintiff first became entitled to remedies arising from the non-performance. Annexure A records the specific obligation and the consequence of non-performance, which the Plaintiff alleges has occurred.

Thereafter, on {{NOTICE_DATE}}, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice demanding the refund of Rs. 2,000,000 along with applicable interest and costs, a copy of which is filed as Annexure C. The Defendant sent a reply on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, the contents of which are referenced in Annexure D. Following these communications, the Plaintiff filed the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} to recover the advance amount and pursue further relief, with the factual basis for the claim anchored in the documentary evidence attached as Annexures A, B, C, and D. The Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 2,000,000, together with interest and costs, as a remedy for the described non-performance and non-refund. Annexure E and Annexure F contain any additional supporting documents relied upon in the action.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a restitutionary obligation whereby a person who has received any advantage under an agreement or contract that is void or that has become void is bound to restore that advantage to the person from whom it was received. In the present case, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} paid Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, as an advance under the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} for a business transaction. The Defendant subsequently defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, resulting in a total failure of consideration; the Defendant has retained the Plaintiff’s money without any lawful justification, and there is no beneﬁt conferred in return for the advance. Accordingly, the Defendant is obliged under Section 65 to restore the amount to the Plaintiff, and the Plaintiff seeks a refund of Rs. 2,000,000 together with such interest thereon and costs as may be lawful. The relief is framed as restitution of the advance with costs, anchored by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document) and Annexure B (Payment proof) to establish the nature of the consideration and the payment made, with Annexure C documenting the legal notice sent to the Defendant and Annexure D the reply, if any. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, Section 65 operates independently of forfeiture clauses, and the failure of consideration—here evidenced by the Defendant’s default—constitutes the ground for restoration of the amount received, regardless of any non-fulfillment of ancillary conditions. The Plaintiff anticipates that the Defendant may contend that no part performance was rendered, that there exists no forfeiture clause, that no adjustment was made, or that there is a counter-claim; none of these defenses defeats the application of Section 65 where the essential facts show receipt of consideration and total non-performance by the Defendant, i.e., the payment of Rs. 2,000,000 was made on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, but the Defendant failed to perform by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. This section’s consequence is restitution to the Plaintiff, the amount paid, with interest and costs as prayed. Annexure A, Annexure B, and the surrounding notice/communication trail corroborate the sequence of events, and Annexure C confirms the legal notice’s dispatch, with Annexure D indicating any reply received.

In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, Article 47 of the Limitation Act governs the period for suits seeking recovery of money paid in pursuance of an existing consideration which later fails, with accrual on the date of failure and a three-year limitation period from that date. Here, the accrual date is {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, being the date on which the cause of action first arose due to the failure of consideration, and the suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}} within the three-year window, thereby preserving the right to seek restitution under the same factual matrix. The Plaintiff acknowledges that there may be arguments about timeliness; however, the evident date of failure and the filed suit within the statutory window sustain the limitation position, and the equitable objective of restoring the Plaintiff’s pre-contract position remains enforceable. The Plaintiff, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, made an advance payment to the Defendant, as evidenced by Annexure B. The Defendant was obligated to perform by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} but failed to repay or perform by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, thereby breaching the contract and giving rise to this action. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}.

Following the breach, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice on {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding payment; the Defendant replied on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}; and the Plaintiff filed this suit on {{FILING_DATE}} seeking recovery of the due amount and costs, with reliance on Annexure C (Legal Notice), Annexure D (Reply, if any), Annexure E (Acknowledgment/Admission, if any), and Annexure F (Additional supporting documents).


JURISDICTION

The advance amount of Rs. 20,00,000 was paid by the Plaintiff on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} by transfer from the Plaintiff's bank account with {{BANK_NAME}} at {{BANK_BRANCH}} to the Defendant's account, as evidenced by Annexure B. The Agreement was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{AGREEMENT_PLACE}}, which lies within the territorial reach of this Court. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} at {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_PLACE}} when the Defendant defaulted in performing its obligation to refund the advance, resulting in total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act. Therefore, this Court has territorial jurisdiction to adjudicate the dispute.

Pecuniary jurisdiction: The Plaintiff seeks Rs. 20,00,000, which falls within the monetary limits of this District Court.

Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}


FACTS OF THE CASE

The Plaintiff and the Defendant came to know each other in the course of pursuing a commercial opportunity, culminating in a formal written agreement between them on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, as evidenced by Annexure A. Their relationship was that of contracting parties to a business transaction, with the Plaintiff proposing and the Defendant evaluating a venture and entering into a binding contract to govern their dealings.

Under the terms of the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff agreed to provide an advance of Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant for the purpose of effecting the business arrangement described in Annexure A. The terms contemplated that, if the transaction could not be carried through, the Defendant would refund the advance to the Plaintiff. The agreement sets out the conditions and remedies for non-performance and references the obligations of both sides to ensure the advance would be used as contemplated or returned if the transaction did not materialize.

The obligations laid down in the contract were that the Plaintiff would disburse the advance amount on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and that the Defendant would perform the required duties and deliver the goods or services by the date specified as {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The parties understood that, in the event the transaction could not be completed, the Defendant would refund the entire advance amount within the period and manner described in the agreement. Annexure A contains the operative terms and the referenced performance timetable.

On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff remitted Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant by bank transfer, as evidenced by the payment proof in Annexure B. The transfer consisted of the full advance amount and was sent to the Defendant in accordance with the details set out in Annexure B, which also includes the date and method of payment. This payment formed the consideration for the contemplated transaction under the Agreement and was intended to be applied to the transaction as described in Annexure A.

By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant did not perform the contractual obligations, and the refund of the advance was not effected. The goods or services were not delivered as contemplated, and the advance money remained with the Defendant without the required return to the Plaintiff as demanded by the terms of the Agreement. The date {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} marks when the Plaintiff first became entitled to remedies arising from the non-performance. Annexure A records the specific obligation and the consequence of non-performance, which the Plaintiff alleges has occurred.

Thereafter, on {{NOTICE_DATE}}, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice demanding the refund of Rs. 2,000,000 along with applicable interest and costs, a copy of which is filed as Annexure C. The Defendant sent a reply on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, the contents of which are referenced in Annexure D. Following these communications, the Plaintiff filed the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} to recover the advance amount and pursue further relief, with the factual basis for the claim anchored in the documentary evidence attached as Annexures A, B, C, and D. The Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 2,000,000, together with interest and costs, as a remedy for the described non-performance and non-refund. Annexure E and Annexure F contain any additional supporting documents relied upon in the action.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a restitutionary obligation whereby a person who has received any advantage under an agreement or contract that is void or that has become void is bound to restore that advantage to the person from whom it was received. In the present case, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} paid Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, as an advance under the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} for a business transaction. The Defendant subsequently defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, resulting in a total failure of consideration; the Defendant has retained the Plaintiff’s money without any lawful justification, and there is no beneﬁt conferred in return for the advance. Accordingly, the Defendant is obliged under Section 65 to restore the amount to the Plaintiff, and the Plaintiff seeks a refund of Rs. 2,000,000 together with such interest thereon and costs as may be lawful. The relief is framed as restitution of the advance with costs, anchored by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document) and Annexure B (Payment proof) to establish the nature of the consideration and the payment made, with Annexure C documenting the legal notice sent to the Defendant and Annexure D the reply, if any. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, Section 65 operates independently of forfeiture clauses, and the failure of consideration—here evidenced by the Defendant’s default—constitutes the ground for restoration of the amount received, regardless of any non-fulfillment of ancillary conditions. The Plaintiff anticipates that the Defendant may contend that no part performance was rendered, that there exists no forfeiture clause, that no adjustment was made, or that there is a counter-claim; none of these defenses defeats the application of Section 65 where the essential facts show receipt of consideration and total non-performance by the Defendant, i.e., the payment of Rs. 2,000,000 was made on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, but the Defendant failed to perform by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. This section’s consequence is restitution to the Plaintiff, the amount paid, with interest and costs as prayed. Annexure A, Annexure B, and the surrounding notice/communication trail corroborate the sequence of events, and Annexure C confirms the legal notice’s dispatch, with Annexure D indicating any reply received.

In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, Article 47 of the Limitation Act governs the period for suits seeking recovery of money paid in pursuance of an existing consideration which later fails, with accrual on the date of failure and a three-year limitation period from that date. Here, the accrual date is {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, being the date on which the cause of action first arose due to the failure of consideration, and the suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}} within the three-year window, thereby preserving the right to seek restitution under the same factual matrix. The Plaintiff acknowledges that there may be arguments about timeliness; however, the evident date of failure and the filed suit within the statutory window sustain the limitation position, and the equitable objective of restoring the Plaintiff’s pre-contract position remains enforceable. Annexure A and Annexure B corroborate the underlying facts of the transaction and the payment, while Annexure C establishes the notice timeline and Annexure D any response thereto.

The Plaintiff also pre-emptively addresses likely defenses: it is categorically asserted that no forfeiture clause or set-off defeats the right to restitution under Section 65, that the sum advanced was a genuine advance for performance under a concrete contract, and that no counter-claim has been raised by the Defendant. To the extent any argument is framed around partial performance, it is determinative that the consideration has failed in its entirety due to the Defendant’s default, and restitution remains the legally appropriate relief. The Plaintiff thus prays for recovery of Rs. 2,000,000 with interest and costs, and the relief sought is consistent with the statutory basis above, with Annexures A through D providing documentary support for the sequence of events.

blocking_issues.issue: Draft contains drafting-note/placeholder line that is not pleading language and may be rejected or create ambiguity if filed as-is
blocking_issues.fix: Delete the line containing "---PLACEHOLDER_TEXT---" and ensure no drafting-note markers remain in the plaint
blocking_issues.location: After “LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS” section (immediately before “CAUSE OF ACTION”)

blocking_issues.issue: Narrative facts are too generic and do not plead the specific transaction timeline/terms and the factual trigger for Section 65 (agreement discovered void / becomes void) with sufficient particularity
blocking_issues.fix: Rewrite the Facts section into a chronological narrative with specific placeholders for: nature of business transaction, key obligations/conditions, what exactly Plaintiff paid and why, what Defendant was required to do by when, how/why the arrangement failed, and how the agreement stood cancelled/became void (e.g., failure of a condition/expiry of time/termination leading to total failure of consideration). Keep legal analysis in the Legal Basis section.
blocking_issues.location: “FACTS OF THE CASE” section; also align supporting averments in “LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS” to plead Section 65 trigger facts

blocking_issues.issue: Inconsistent and confusing accrual/limitation anchoring (DEFAULT_DATE vs CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE) creates limitation risk and internal inconsistency
blocking_issues.fix: Use a single accrual anchor consistent with Article 47 (date of failure of consideration) and plead it consistently across Jurisdiction (as applicable), Cause of Action, Limitation, and Legal Basis. Remove/replace conflicting references to {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} where used as accrual date; treat procedural steps (notice/reply) only as subsequent events, not the limitation anchor.
blocking_issues.location: Introductory factual paragraph; “JURISDICTION” (cause of action line); “LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS” (Article 47 paragraph); “CAUSE OF ACTION”; “LIMITATION”

blocking_issues.issue: Unconditional factual assertions about notice reply/acknowledgment are made without confirmed facts (risk of pleading incorrect facts if not true)
blocking_issues.fix: Qualify such assertions as conditional (e.g., “reply, if any”) and/or use clear placeholders; avoid stating a reply/acknowledgment occurred unless the Plaintiff confirms it
blocking_issues.location: Paragraph beginning “Following the breach, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice…”; references to Annexure D/E

non_blocking_issues.issue: Prayer paragraph is drafted as a single continuous paragraph with multiple sub-prayers; improves clarity and filing-readiness if broken into separate numbered sub-prayers with consistent interest period start-date
non_blocking_issues.fix: Format prayers as separate sub-paragraphs (a), (b), (c)… each on its own line; align pre-suit interest start date with the pleaded default/failure date (unless claiming from payment date with a stated basis)

non_blocking_issues.issue: Claim amount is expressed inconsistently (template placeholders in title vs fixed numerals elsewhere; also Rs. 20,00,000 vs 2,000,000)
non_blocking_issues.fix: Use one consistent representation throughout (prefer Rs. 20,00,000/- and words) and ensure title and body match

non_blocking_issues.issue: Minor terminology inconsistency (“Opposite Party”) in a civil money recovery plaint may be replaced with “Defendant” for standard practice
Legal analysis is lightly mixed into the Jurisdiction section (reference to Section 65)
non_blocking_issues.fix: Keep Jurisdiction confined to territorial/pecuniary facts; move Section 65 mention to Legal Basis (optional stylistic improvement)

unsupported_statements.reason: No case-specific facts were provided confirming that a reply was actually sent; the draft should plead “reply, if any” unless confirmed.
unsupported_statements.location: Paragraph beginning “Following the breach, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice…”

unsupported_statements.statement: “no counter-claim has been raised by the Defendant”
unsupported_statements.reason: Whether a counter-claim has been raised is procedural and case-stage dependent; at filing stage, it is not a fact within the plaint record unless the suit is already at a later stage.
unsupported_statements.location: “LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS” (paragraph beginning “The Plaintiff also pre-emptively addresses likely defenses…”)
